coredata.dc:title: Usage of coconut coir for sustainable production of high-valued carbon dots with discriminatory sensing aptitude toward metal ions
coredata.dc:creator: Chauhan, P.
Dogra, S.
Chaudhary, S.
Kumar, R.
coredata.dc:description: A novel, remunerative, green synthetic methodology without adding any kind of extra chemical reagent has been proposed for synthesizing highly fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) by using waste coconut coir as the C source. The as-prepared C-dots possessed a quantum yield of 48% and displayed a high solubility rate in aqueous media. The fluorescence emission intensity of C-dots is found to be excitation dependent with high stability toward variation in the reaction medium. The bio-congenial performance of C-dots has been checked by employing quantitative multi-assay including fungus, plant, aquatic animals, and at the chromosomal level. It is notable that the antibacterial studies of as-prepared C-dots have presented appealing outcomes which advocated that C-dots prepared using coconut coir are biocompatible toward both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria with no zone of inhibition in much higher concentration ranges. The as-prepared C-dots provided a simple sensing podium with high sensitivity and selectivity with a “turn-on and turn-off” fluorescence response for the detection of dual metal ions i.e. cadmium and copper ions in aqueous media with a detection limit of 0.18 nM and 0.28 nM, respectively. The practical utilities of the biocompatible C-dots have been checked over different water resources. The current work provided a simple, easy, bio-matched, harmless, rapid, and cost-effective sensory probe for wastewater remediation.

(a) XRD pattern, (b) FTIR spectrum, (c) HRTEM image, and (d) DLS spectrum of as-synthesized C-dots.

(a) Pictorial representation of the seed germination behavior of Vigna radiata seeds in the presence of different concentrations of C-dots; (b) and (c) show the effects of concentration of C-dots on root length, percentage inhibition growth, germination index, and germination percentage values for Vigna radiata seeds.

(a) Pictorial representation of different stages in the root tips of Allium cepa in the presence of different concentrations of C-dots, (b) effect of concentration of C-dots on MI, RMI, and CAI values of Allium cepa.

head.ce:title: Usage of coconut coir for sustainable production of high-valued carbon dots with discriminatory sensing aptitude toward metal ions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel, remunerative, green synthetic methodology without adding any kind of extra chemical reagent has been proposed for synthesizing highly fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) by using waste coconut coir as the C source. The as-prepared C-dots possessed a quantum yield of 48% and displayed a high solubility rate in aqueous media. The fluorescence emission intensity of C-dots is found to be excitation dependent with high stability toward variation in the reaction medium. The bio-congenial performance of C-dots has been checked by employing quantitative multi-assay including fungus, plant, aquatic animals, and at the chromosomal level. It is notable that the antibacterial studies of as-prepared C-dots have presented appealing outcomes which advocated that C-dots prepared using coconut coir are biocompatible toward both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria with no zone of inhibition in much higher concentration ranges. The as-prepared C-dots provided a simple sensing podium with high sensitivity and selectivity with a “turn-on and turn-off” fluorescence response for the detection of dual metal ions i.e. cadmium and copper ions in aqueous media with a detection limit of 0.18 nM and 0.28 nM, respectively. The practical utilities of the biocompatible C-dots have been checked over different water resources. The current work provided a simple, easy, bio-matched, harmless, rapid, and cost-effective sensory probe for wastewater remediation.

ce:section.ce:para: The environmental depreciation via the toxic impact of heavy metal ions has turned out to be a major concern due to their harsh impact on our bionomics [1,2]. Out of different types of existing metal ions, the lethal impact of cadmium(II) (Cd2+) and copper(II) (Cu2+) ions has produced severe damage to our ecosystem and human health due to their higher rate of stability [3,4]. The overexposure to copper metal ion could produce rigorous harm to our nervous system and lead to various neurodegenerative diseases like Wilson's disease and Alzheimer’s disease [5,6]. In addition, the overexposure to Cd2+ ions leads to diseases like “itai-itai”, failure of liver and kidney, and lethal impact on reproductive, immune, and respiratory systems of living beings [7,8]. Simultaneously, Cd2+ ions are considered to have high inclination to intercommunicate with thiol groups of proteins and form highly stable and poisonous cadmium–metallothionein complexes in living systems [9,10]. Therefore, the detection of the respective ion from different types of water resources with high selectivity and sensitivity is congregating so much consideration among researchers [9,11]. To date, various available analytical techniques such as atomic absorption spectroscopy, potentiometric techniques, and inductively coupled plasmon resonance strategies have been employed for sensing the existence of metal ions. However, the main difficulties associated with these techniques are their hefty procedural cost with preliminary time-consuming preparation processing and requirement of professional expertise for handling sophisticated instruments [12–15].

In contrast to available techniques, the fluorescence spectrophotometry-based methodology has been found to be quite effective for metal ion sensing due to its high selectivity and sensitivity and low procedural expenditure [16–18]. Out of different types of conventional fluorophores, carbon-based nanodots are considered one of the potential sensory advanced materials. The existence of high stability toward various reaction medium, photostability, and biocompatible nature with excellent solubility in aqueous media made C-dots superior contender for the diverse range of applications in environmental remediation. In the literature, Wang et al. have synthesized C-dots by utilizing a chemical oxidation process and liquid fuels as a precursor. The developed C-dots show definitive discrimination toward copper ion and the value of detection limit has been found to be 0.039 μM [19]. Gu et al. adapted lily bulbs as a predecessor material for the synthesis of C-dots by using microwave-assisted analysis. The value of developed C-dots has been reported to be 17.6%. The developed C-dots show exceptional selectivity and sensitivity toward copper metal with 26 nm value of detection limit [20]. Niu et al. prepared carbon quantum dot/AuNC nanohybrids by applying a chemical synthetic method. The developed systems show ratiometric fluorescent sensing toward cadmium ion. The value of detection limit has been reported to be 32.5 nM [21]. Li et al. have also prepared nitrogen doped C-dots–graphene oxide hybrid by applying microwave synthesis. The developed hybrid system was found to be electrochemically sensitive and selective toward cadmium ion. The value of detection limit has been found to be 7.45 μg/ml [22]. The major problems associated with the above-mentioned methods are their time-consuming synthesis along with expensive methods and lower value of detection limit and their approach toward biocompatibility and toxicity has not been explored. The conventional synthetic processes such as laser ablation, hydrothermal, microwave, electrochemical, and pyrolysis methods have been employed for the production of C-dots. However, the multistep processing and expensive procedural cost have displayed a major hitch in the popularity of these methods during the production of C-dots. Therefore, researchers from different fields are now applying great efforts in developing fairly easy and economical methodologies for large-scale production of C-dots [23–26]. In this regard, the application of greener precursors such as seeds of wheat, rice, barley, grasses, banana, and orange juice has fulfilled the quest for producing economical viable C-dots [27–31].

Coconut coir is one of such starting precursors, which can be employed for the fabrication of C-dots. The coconut coir is obtained from the exterior thick mesocarp of the coconut fruit with approximately 75% of fibrous component as its major constituent [32,33]. In addition, the presence of lignin, cellulose, and hemicelluloses in coconut coir has signified its utilization as a starting precursor for the synthesis of C-dots. The usage of such bulk carbon precursor for the preparation of C-dots has overcome the disposal problem of this waste coir to form useful fluorescent probes for the selective and sensitive detection of metal ions. Therefore, the current work has proposed the novel, remunerative, green synthetic methodology without adding any kind of extra chemical reagent for developing highly fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) by using waste coconut coir as the carbon source. The quantum yield and fluorescence emission intensity have been estimated in aqueous media. The bio-congenial (biocompatibility) performance of as-prepared C-dots has also been analyzed by using quantitative multi-assay approaches via different bacterial and fungus strains. Simultaneously, the different other biocompatibility assessment approaches have been performed for C-dots on algal species, plants (Vigna radiata seeds), and at the chromosomal level (Allium cepa) which shows their enhanced embellishment in the medical field also. The fluorescence emission properties of as-prepared C-dots have further been employed as a simple sensing podium for the detection of metal ions with high sensitivity and selectivity in aqueous media. In view of our observation, this is one of the primary testimonies where application of coconut coir has been utilized as a renewable starting precursor for the fabrication of highly fluorescent C-dots. Additionally, the outstanding PL activities exhibited by biocompatible C-dots in different pH have also been studied and that would endorse the fabrication strategy and usage of such waste biomass-derived C-dots as long-range emissive fluorophoric probes for metal ions sensing. The promising results from various water resources explored their utility in practical application. The current work provided a simple, easy, bio-matched, harmless, rapid, and cost-effective sensory probe for wastewater remediation.

Coconut coir has been purchased from Sector-15 Chandigarh local market. All the used metal salts of chloride and nitrate (barium, copper, cadmium, zinc, potassium, sodium, silver, calcium, magnesium, iron, lead, ammonium, and mercury) have been purchased from Sigma-Aldrich with 99% purity. Muller Hinton Agar, Agar-Agar type I, malt extract powder, and nutrient broth have been purchased from HiMedia with 98% purity for antimicrobial and antifungal activity. Chlorella algal sp. was procured from the Department of Botany, Panjab University (P.U), Chandigarh (Chd.) and allowed to grow in BBM solution. BBM solution was procured from HiMedia with 99% purity. Laurilia taxodii fungus has been obtained from the Department of Environmental Science, P.U., Chd. The respective bacterial strains viz. Escherichia coli, Staphylococcus aureus, Salmonella typhi, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa were acquired from the Department of Microbiology, P.U., Chd. Methylene blue dye and mercury chloride have been procured from Emplura and Fisher Scientific. Sodium hydroxide pellets and conc. HCl were from Fisher Scientific with 99% purity. Deionized water was utilized for all experiments. All the chemicals were used as such without any further purification.

The thermal calcination process was adopted for the preparation of C-dots from coconut coir. The choice of the method was based on the multiple step reactions like decarboxylation, hydrolysis, polymerisation, and dehydration in the hydrothermal process. For the synthesis, coconut coir was cut into small pieces using a sterilized knife and then grounded well to make fine powder. The coir powder (20 g) was taken in a silica crucible and placed in a muffle furnace in nitrogen atmosphere at 600 °C for 3 h (Fig. 1 ). The silica crucible with brown color powder has been removed from the furnace after cooling. The obtained powder was crumbled well to make fine particles of carbon. Then, 0.5 g of crumbled fine particles have further been dispersed in 100 ml of deionized water and kept under stirring for 18 h. The as-obtained solution has further allowed centrifuging for 8 min at 5000 rpm for the removal of aggregated form of particles. The supernatant of the respective sample was further filtered and preserved under room temperature for carrying out analysis.

An AICIL muffle furnace has been used for carrying out the thermic insulated course of action during the synthesis of C-dots. The ultra-centrifugation has been done using a REMI R-24 centrifuge. An IKA C-MAG HS7 magnetic stirrer with a heating plate has been used for the stirring of samples. The absorption performance of the C-dots has been checked by LABINDIA UV–visible spectroscopy. Emission response of C-dots has been assessed by employing a Perkin Elmer LS 55. The fluorescent quantum yield of the synthesized particles has been calculated by taking Rhodamine B as a reference material in dilute basic ethanol media. For the reference, Rhodamine B has displayed the quantum yield (
φ
R
) value of 0.65. For the analysis, the solution of the respective dye has been prepared in basic ethanol solution. The formula used for the calculation of quantum yield is given below:
(1)

where X corresponds to the sample (C-dot solution), R corresponds to the reference material, ɳ is the refractive index of solution, A refers to the optical density values calculated from absorption spectra and I refers to the integrated emission intensity of the sample. A high precision gas–vapor volumetric adsorption instrument (MictrotracBEL Corp. and Belprep vacIII) has been used to determine the specific surface area, pore size distribution, and pore volume of C-dots. The hydrodynamic radial profile of the developed C-dots has been estimated using a dynamic light scattering instrument (MALVERN ZEN 1690). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, Perkin Elmer (RX1) instrument has been deployed to test the presence of different functional groups over the surface of developed C-dots in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. To determine the purity and elemental composition of developed C-dots, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was carried out using an FE-SEM instrument (JEOL JSM 7500F) at 20 keV. High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) has been used to scrutinize the direct imaging of atomic structure and morphological alteration in C-dots. A Panalytical X'Pert Pro powder X-ray diffractometer under monochromatic CuKα radiation has been used to check the nanocrystalline structure of C-dots in-between 5° and 90° angles at a scan rate of 2°/min. The thermal characteristic properties of C-dots have been investigated on a differential scanning calorimeter, METTLER TOLEDO (Q20) instrument. A NIKON ECLIPSE E100 optical light microscope has been used to check the genotoxicity assessment or chromosomal behavior of A. Cepa in the presence of C-dots. A Mettler Toledo digital pH meter has been used to explore the influential effect of pH over synthesized particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studying the effect of C-dots on the growth of Vigna radiata seeds
ce:section.ce:para:
Before applying the as-synthesized C-dots in the agricultural sector, it is mandatory to evaluate the potential toxicity of as-prepared C-dots. For the analysis, Vigna radiata seeds were used to check the influential behavior of different concentrations of C-dots ranging from 500 to 5000 ppm on germination activities of seeds. For the analysis, the respective seeds were purchased from a local market of Sector-15 Chandigarh and were sterilized by washing with HgCl2 solution for 5 min. To remove the excess of undesired form of HgCl2, seeds were washed with triply distilled water for 10 min. The respective petri plate activity was performed to study the emergence of seedlings and their different growth parameters in the presence of different concentrations of C-dots. The growth of seedlings was observed after 48 h. One control system was also set up for the evaluation of standard parameters [34–36]. After four days of exposure to the seeds under C-dots, the emergence percentage and germination index values have been calculated using the following equation:
(2)

percentage

Out of total, number of germinated seeds

where Nt represents the total number of germinated seeds for each system after four days of exposure, Lt represents the average of root length of germinated seeds for each system, Nc represents the total number of germinated seeds for the control system, and Lc corresponds to the average of root length of germinated seeds for the control system. The percentage inhibition growth of the seeds in the presence of C-dots has been calculated using the following equation:
(4)

Inhibition

where LC is the average length in control sprouts and LN is the average length in C-dots solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A. cepa test to study the genotoxic effect of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para:
A. cepa chromosomal aberration assay was performed to investigate the detailed toxicity profiling of synthesized C-dots at different concentration levels ranging from 100 to 5000 ppm. For the experimentation, freshly grown onions of equal weight and size were purchased from a local market of Sector-15 Chandigarh. The onion bulbs were washed with triply distilled water for 2 min after removing outer 2–3 layers with the help of a sterilized knife. The extra root tips were removed by using a pair of sterile scissors, for the growth of new root tips. After the initial treatment, the processed onion bulbs were placed in freshly prepared solutions of C-dot of different concentrations for three days, under normal light and room temperature. For the control experiment, onion bulbs were dipped in deionized water. After three days of incubation period, onion bulbs were removed from the beaker and washed with distilled water. Then, fresh root tips were cut with the help of a pair of sterilized scissors and placed on a glass slide. The root tips were firstly washed with 1 N HCl. The methylene blue dye was applied to stain the root tips for 3–4 min. The excess amount of the dye was removed by washing with deionized water for about 4–5 times. The cover slip was placed over the root tip very carefully to prevent the formation of air bubble. The prepared slides were studied under a light microscope (NIKON ECLIPSE 450) to observe the different genetic levels in root tips. The same experiment was performed in triplicate for different concentrations of C-dots for the justification of obtained data and removing the error. The different stages of mitotic divisions (prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase, and interphase) were counted and respective chromosomal aberrations were used to calculate the values of mitotic index and other related parameters using the following equations [37–39].
(5)
Mitotic Index
(
MI
)
=
P + M + A + T
Total number of cells in interphase
(6)

(

where P, M, A, T, and I correspond to prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase, and interphase. TC, TDC, and MIc correspond to the count of individual phase, count of PMAT, and index of mitotic division for the control standard sample. CA refers to the count of each aberration and TD refers to the total number of divisions.

The antimicrobial activity of C-dots was checked using agar well diffusion assay with inhibitory zone assessment in the presence of five different concentrations of C-dots, 5000, 2500, 1000, 500, and 100 ppm. Four bacterial strains of different nature viz. Escherichia coli (Gram negative), Salmonella typhi (Gram negative), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Gram negative), and Staphylococcus aureus (Gram positive) were utilized during the analysis. The nutrient broth was used for the growth of bacterium culture. The 50 μl/ml concentration of bacteria and C-dot solutions were taken for the analysis. The deionized water was used as a standard as it has shown a wide variety of activity toward both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. Muller Hinton agar was used as a predecessor for the growth of bacterium. For the measurement, agar solution was autoclaved to sterilize the conditions. A Petri plate was used for the solidification and gel formation in autoclaved agar. To maintain the sanitized condition, laminar airflow was used to apply bacteria over agar gel. For the analysis of samples, wells of uniform size were made with the help of 5 mm needle. The agar plates were incubated at 37 °C in an incubator for 24 h for obtaining the effective zone of inhibition [40–42]. To check reliability of bacterial strains over C-dots, the performed experiment was repeated for three times. The percent reduction of bacterial population (P%) was calculated using the following equation:
(9)
P % =
M − N
M
× 100
where M and N correspond to the population of bacteria in the control sample and in the presence of C-dots.

Laurilia taxodii fungal species was utilized to study the antifungal activities of C-dots. The fungus strain was maintained in a liquid nutrient broth solution containing yeast extract, peptone, dextrose, beef extract, and sodium chloride as the solid extract. The fungal strain was further stored at 18 °C, inside the incubator to maintain the sterilized condition. For the maximum growth of fungus, media of Agar-Agar1 and malt extract were utilized during the analysis. C-dots of different concentrations were autoclaved with fungus media and allowed to stand for 24 h to form gel [43,44]. A pure fungus culture in the form of a small disc was applied to the fungus media and kept for two days in an incubator at 26 °C. The spot stationary assay and radial inhibition growth assay were used to apply fungal culture by using sterilized loop and fungal disc cutter. The value of the antifungal index has been calculated using the following equation:
(10)
Antifungal Index
(
%
)
=
(
1 -
D
a
D
b
)
×
100
where Da and Db stand for the growth zones in the test plate and control plate, respectively.

The anti-algal toxicity assessment experiment has been performed to check the biocompatibility of as-fabricated C-dots over water species and its in situ application. The algal paper disc method on micro flora incorporation with algal species (Chlorella sp.) was used for the measurement. The freshly prepared re-cultured solution of algae was used during the study. For re-culturing, BBM solution was placed under dark:light exposure for 16:8 h for one week using a Philips LED bulb inside a cupboard. The thoroughly dried and autoclaved natural loamy sand soil with 90% sand, 8% slit and 2% clay was used for sample preparation. A Whatman filter paper was utilized for making paper disc with an approximate diameter of 5 mm [45,46]. The different concentration solutions of C-dots were prepared in BBM solution to maintain the solvent properties. A 96-well tissue culture plate was utilized for the experimentation. Firstly, wells present inside the culture plate were one third filled with natural loamy soil. Then freshly prepared solution of C-dots (100 μl solution) was poured in a culture plate. After that, paper discs were placed in each well with the help of a sterilized loop. Then, freshly re-cultured algae suspension of 10 μl was dispersed over a paper disc. The well tissue culture plate was covered properly and placed under 16:8 h of light and dark sequence for 10 days. The chlorophyll content was quantified by UV–visible spectroscopy. To check the efficiency of synthesized C-dots over algal species, the same experiment was repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ion sensing application of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the application of developed C-dots in sensing of metal ions, fluorescence spectroscopy has been used to check the emission behavior of C-dots after adding different types of metal ion salts of nitrate (Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, K+, NH4 +, Ag+, Na+, Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+). The sensitivity of our system has been studied under room temperature using deionized water as a medium toward assorted metal ions. During this specific study, the pH was maintained at 8, and an apposite quantity of C-dot solution was used. The photoluminescence spectrum of C-dots was recorded after adding an equal and exact amount of metal ions (5 μM) to C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and morphological characterization of C-dots prepared using coconut coir
ce:section.ce:para: It was inferred from the spectra that on increasing the pH from 2 to 8, the emission intensity of C-dots has started increasing gradually with maxima at pH = 8. This was mainly due to the stability of synthesized C-dots in slight alkaline media. The emission intensity has shown significant decrease on enhancing the pH of the reaction media (pH > 8). The abruption in the emission behavior has been aroused due to the change in the acidic behavior of C-dots to the basic nature. This variation has caused the removal or addition of proton part to the different carboxyl and amine moieties present on the exterior surface of C-dots and led to change in the electrostatic interacting behavior (Fig. 2b). The attenuate solution of C-dots has shown blue coloration under UV-light, whereas transparent color was seen under normal light condition (Fig. 2c inset). To ensure the optical properties of synthesized C-dots, their absorption spectrum has also been recorded in the range of 200–700 nm (Fig. 2c). In absorption spectrum, two characteristic peaks observed at 270 nm and 390 nm which corresponds to the π–π* and n–π* transition of aromatic ring and present C=C, C=O and hydroxyl groups (-OH) groups. The presence of these peak further supports the water sustainable properties of C-dots [47].

The thermal behavior of as-prepared C-dots has been verified by using the DSC profiling of C-dots (Fig. S1a). The existence of two exothermic peaks with the values of heat of enthalpy and transition temperature as 5.69, 16.47 J/g and 96.66, 104.18 °C, respectively, has been shown for the prepared C-dots. The onset temperature (T0) and full width at half maxima temperature (T1/2) was found to be 91.87 °C and 6.79 °C, respectively. The melting temperature (Tm) for as-prepared C-dots was around 98.98 °C. The value of entropy (ΔS) and Gibbs free energy (ΔG) was found to be 0.0575 J/K and 0.135 KJ/mol−1, respectively. The positive value of ΔG has further suggested the endergonic and non-spontaneous behavior of C-dots. The strong adsorption ability with non-porous nature has been verified from the adsorption and desorption isotherm by using N2 physisorption analysis for C-dots (Fig. S1b–d). The adsorption temperature and saturated vapor pressure have been kept at 77 K and 98.88 KPa during the analysis. The mean pore diameter of C-dots has been found to be 10.97 nm with a correlation coefficient of 0.9966. The values of total pore volume and adsorption cross-section area were found to be 6.76E-03 cm3/g and 0.162 nm2, respectively. For the samples, type IV adsorption isotherm (monolayer followed by multilayer) with the presence of hysteresis loop of type H3 has also been observed in C-dots. The results have been explained on the basis of condensation of different gases in the small capillary pores of the adsorbent material at a pressure below saturated pressure of gas. The existence of different functional groups over the surface of C-dots has the ability to move toward the inside surface of pore with provision of the diffusion process into the pores and attain the active site on the surface of particles [48]. Elemental dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis was utilized to investigate the purity of developed C-dots (Fig. S2). From the EDX spectrum, it was concluded that the developed C-dots contain carbon and oxygen in highly abundant forms whereas nitrogen was in trace amounts. As carbon has highest proportion (89%) in the developed C-dots, the origin of strong C lies between 3 and 4 keV. Simultaneously, an elemental peak was also observed for oxygen between 0 and 1 keV. From here, it can be concluded that, developed C-dots have only required elemental peaks, which confirms the purity of developed C-dots. An X-ray diffraction (XRD) study has been performed to evaluate the crystalline nature of C-dots (Fig. 3 a). The peaks are present at 2θ = 28.2, 40.4, 50.2, 58.54, and 66.37 and are in (002), (111), (200), (600), and (220) diffracting planes in C-dots. In addition, the existence of small peaks has represented that the growth of C-dots has occurred in every direction with a crystalline size of the order of 12.5 nm. The dislocation density (ʆ) was found to be 0.0064, which has further associated with the probability of defects present in the sample.

The Williamson-Hall method has been applied for the detailed structural analysis of stress, strain, and amount of energy density in C-dots keeping the XRD spectrum into consideration. The uniform deformation model (UDM) has been applied to explore the strain-induced broadening in C-dots. The uniform stress deformation model (USDM) has been applied to check the deformation stress in C-dots. The uniform deformation energy density model (UDEDM) has been used to evaluate the amount of energy density of the C-dots and the size–strain plot (SSP) model has been used to calculate the value of RMS strain. For the analysis, the corresponding value of intercept has been considered negligible here. All these respective values have been calculated from the slope of obtained XRD curves (Table 1 ). Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy has been performed to understand the nature of functional group on the surface of C-dots (Fig. 3b). The characteristic absorption peak at 2960 cm−1 and sharp narrow peak around 1450 cm−1 have represented the existence of C–H stretching and –CH2 bending modes in C-dots. The short band near 1106 cm−1 has been attributed to the C–O stretching. The typical absorption peaks around 870 and 650 cm−1 represent the strong = C–H bending and aromatic sp2 C–H bending in C-dots. The HRTEM analysis has been used to explain the morphological alterations and size distribution profile of synthesized C-dots. From the obtained results, it was concluded that the synthesized particles are monodispersed and spherical in nature having a size less than 10 nm (Fig. 3c). The corresponding value of hydrodynamic radii of as-synthesized C-dots has been found to be 10 nm with a polydispersity index (PDI) value of 0.3 (Fig. 3d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of C-dots on the growth of Vigna radiata seeds
ce:section.ce:para: The significant effect of C-dots on seed germination has been explained on the basis of chemical nature, size, and shape of chosen particles. The corresponding nature of surface and surface area of chosen particles has also played a vital role in the phytotoxic activity of germinated seeds. To visualize this effect, the growth of Vigna radiata seeds have has been investigated in five different concentrations of C-dots ranging from 100 ppm to 5000 ppm (Fig. 4 a). The observation parameters including germination percentage, germination index and inhibition growth of nuclei have been investigated as a function of concentration in the presence of C-dots. Form the data, it has been found that the germination percentage was almost 100% for each seed in a chosen range of concentration. In addition, the significant increment in the values of germination index and root length has further supported the growth of nuclei in Vigna radiata seeds in the presence of C-dots. The value of average root length was found to be comparable in all cases but maximum growth was obtained in case of 2500 and 5000 ppm. The germination of seeds was accelerated in all concentration of C-dots with respect to the control system. The value of germination index was found to be maximum in the case of 2500 ppm, which is 103% due to the significant increment of water absorption capacity of Vigna radiata seeds. The value of percentage inhibition growth (PIG) was found to be negligible in almost all cases. The enhancement in biocompatibility was found to be more than 100% in some concentrations with respect to the control standard. From the graph, it can concluded that the synthesized C-dots are highly biocompatible in nature and has shown strong consistency over different trials. The non-toxic nature of C-dots has preferred their use in the agricultural field for the enhancement of rootlets (Fig. 4b and c).

The A. cepa assay has been chosen in the current methodology due to its high sensitivity in verifying the effect of different concentrations of C-dots on the genotoxic effect (Fig. 5 ). A. cepa has been considered one of the significant plant species to evaluate different types of xenobiotic characteristics of C-dots in detail. The clear visualization in the variations of chromosomal structure and count of number of cells in A. cepa has provided the direct evidence of the toxicity level induced in the presence of C-dots. In addition, the morphological alteration of different stages in the growth cycle of A. cepa further represented the behavior of C-dots on the growth pattern (Fig. 5). The different mitotic stages presented in the root tips of A. cepa in the presence of different concentrations of C-dots have displayed the significant variation behavior. Table S1 recapitulated the effect of different concentrations of C-dots on root tips of A. cepa and on the values of phase index, MI, RMI and CAI values in the presence of different concentrations of C-dots. Form the data, it has further been found that the obtained values are approximately the same at every concentration of C-dots with respect to the control system. The results have supported the biocompatible nature of C-dots. However, the percentage of chromosomal aberration was found to be negligible in all concentrations except 5000 ppm. This behavioral aspect has been explained due to the existence of sticky chromosomes in cells at 5000 ppm of C-dots. These morphological alterations from normal states have produced slight toxicity to the A. cepa species. Moreover, the stickiness might have been produced further due to the entanglement of interchromosomal chromatin fibers and produced little toxic behavior at this concentration. The presence of nanoparticles can explicate their actions depending on the size and shape of the particle. The applied concentration, specific conditions of experiments, and mechanism of uptake of C-dots have further supported their influence over the A. cepa. In this study, the good photo stability, surface properties, and high quantum yield of as-prepared C-dots from waste coconut coir have enhanced the activity of C-dots toward A. cepa. The small sizes of C-dots (>10 nm in this case) made their easy adsorption in the root-meristems of A. cepa. The respective absorption process of C-dots blocks the formation of reactive oxygen species and further leads to the lower chromosomal aberration. The biocompatible nature of C-dots further supports the different genetic levels of A. cepa along with the DNA and does not cause any chromosomal aberrations. In addition, the non-toxic behavior of functional moieties over the surface of C-dots have not affected the redox homeostasis reaction of the cell wall of A. cepa and further supported their growth.

In the current study, the agar well diffusion method has been used to verify the antimicrobial activity of C-dots by using commonly available bacteria inside and outside our body system. To test the antimicrobial activity of C-dots, four different types of bacterial strains viz. Escherichia coli (Gram negative), Staphylococcus aureus (Gram positive), Salmonella typhi (Gram negative), and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (Gram negative) have been chosen for the model species. Fig. S3 has explained the corresponding effect of different concentrations of C-dots over the inhibition zone of chosen bacterial strains. The high water solubility and biocompatible nature of C-dots due to the presence of different types of functional groups (hydroxyl, amine, and carboxyl) over the exterior surface have further augmented their applicability in studying their potential in antibacterial activities against different types of bacterial strains. These functional moieties over the surface of C-dots have prevented the formation of chemically reactive oxygen species like superoxide and hydroxyl radicals in the system. The non-formation of free radical species in the presence of C-dots has produced no negative effect over the growth of the bacterial strains. The as-synthesized C-dots also displayed high stability and good conductivity in bacterial system. This type of behavior has been explained on the basis of nature and thickness of the cell wall of bacterial strain. The presence of different types of peptidoglycan and polysaccharides of lipid units in the outer cell wall and cytoplasm in the inner layer has further influenced their growth in the presence of C-dots. The cell wall of Gram-positive bacteria is around 30–70 nm and of Gram-negative bacteria is around 10–15 nm. The value of percentage reduction of bacterial strain was found to be zero in each concentration of C-dot solution. The cell walls of bacterial strains have been found to be non-ruptured in the presence of C-dots. The enhancement in biocompatibility has been found to be exactly the same for all concentrations of C-dots as compared to the control system for all bacterial strains. In addition, the presence of C-dots has promoted the growth of bacterial strains in the presence of different concentrations of C-dots. From here, it has been concluded that the synthesized C-dots do not show any value of zone of inhibition in any kind of bacterial strain. Hence, the formed particles were found to be non-toxic toward bacterial strains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of C-dots on the growth behavior of Laurilia taxodii and Chlorella algal species
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of different concentrations ranging from 100 ppm to 5000 ppm of C-dots has been taken to ensure the antifungal and anti-algal activities on Laurilia taxodii and Chlorella algal species, respectively. The obtained results display the biocompatible nature of the synthesized C-dots (Fig. 6 a and b). On interpreting the data, it has been found that the concentration of C-dots has produced a slight reduction in the growth of the fungus (Fig. 6a) as compared to the control. The value of the antifungal index has been represented in Fig. 6b. The enhancement in the concentration of C-dots has produced the increment in the mobility of ions due to high conducting and photochemical stability. The mobile ions have the ability to travel fast and have the tendency to enter inside the peripheral wall of fungus and affect the main cell wall component of fungus i.e. chitin (derivative of glucose). The disruption process of chitin molecules has the direct influence over the mycelium growth of fungus. As a result, the lower concentration of C-dots has more biocompatibility toward the chosen fungal species.

The effect of same concentration of C-dots has also been studied over Chlorella algal species as one of the representatives of aquatic system in the current studies. The choice of the algal species has been based on the uniform and definite cell structure of Chlorella algal species as compared to other. Chlorella algal species has also possessed high chloroplast content. The chosen Chlorella algal species also possessed the multicellular nature and has prokaryotic organisms inside where DNA is enclosed by a nuclear membrane forming a nucleus cell in the species. For the analysis, the respective amount of the chlorophyll content has been evaluated by applying the paper-disc method as a function of concentration of C-dots (Fig. 6c). The measurement of the chlorophyll content has provided a direct evidence of the phyla-genetic process in the species as it gives the idea about the promotion of oxygen through the photosynthesis process. The quantitative amount of chlorophyll content has concluded that there has been a slight variation of chlorophyll value as a function of concentration of C-dots. This behavioral variation has been explained on the basis of the influence of C-dots over the light harvesting complex formation ability of algal species. The C-dots of lower concentration have comparable light-harvesting tendency as those of the control system. From here, it can be concluded that the synthesized C-dots do not restrict the growth of algae in lower concentration and are found to be a biocompatible tool toward aquatic species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ion sensing aptitude of C-dots prepared using coconut coir
ce:section.ce:para: The well-defined emission properties of C-dots have recommended us to exploit their potential application in the field of sensing especially selective sensing of metal ions. The presence of metal ions has produced imperative character in the physiological performance of existed organisms at a particular level. After exceeding the particular concentration, different types of diseases like typhoid, worm disease, dysentery, guinea, cholera, and diarrhea have aroused in living beings. The main source of intake of various metal ions is our drinking water. Therefore, it is very much important to detect trace amount of these toxic metal ions by using a simple and sensitive detection methodology. In this paper, the emission properties of C-dots were utilized for selective sensing of metal ions. For the analysis, the fluorescence emission behavior of C-dots has been studied in the presence of 5 μM solution of Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Cd2+, K+, NH4 +, Ag+, Na+, Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ ions under room temperature condition (Fig. 7 a). The deionized water has been chosen as a medium toward assorted nitrate salt of different types of metal ions. The pH of the reaction media has been maintained at 8 with 0.5 g of C-dots in 100 ml of water. The presence of cadmium ion has displayed the enhancement in the fluorescence emission intensity as compared to the C-dots in the absence of any metal ion. However, the existence of copper ion has shown significant quenching of fluorescence emission intensity of C-dot. The other metal ions have displayed no significant change in the emission intensity. The outcomes have clearly depicted that our developed C-dots displayed turn-on sensor for cadmium ion and turn-off sensor for copper ion (Fig. 7a).

The enhancement and quenching of fluorescence emission intensity have been prominently caused due to the variations in the environmental and physical changes in the C-dots in the presence of Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions. The quenching in fluorescence emission intensity of C-dots in the presence of Cu2+ ions has mainly aroused due to the strong association of Cu2+ ions with the hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amine group over the surface of C-dots. The steady state chelate complex formation tendency of C-dot and copper ion has further stimulated the variations in the environment of C-dots and has further grouped the elementary charges in between the bonded atoms. This strong association between C-dots and Cu2+ ions has led to the elevation of non-radiative recombination of charges in C-dots and produced intensity quenching. Another reason of quenching might be physical interaction of C-dot and copper metal ion, which has produced hindrance of plasmon coupling and electric field distribution in the fluorescence intensity (Scheme 1 ). The intensity behavior has further been tested as a function of concentration of Cu2+ ions in the presence of C-dots. From the graph, it has been inferred that on decreasing the concentration of copper from 0.001 μM to 5 nM, the luminescence intensity of C-dots has produced enhancement in the peak intensity (Fig. 7b). The splitting of the peak was not observed on varying concentration from 0.001 μM to 5 μM. This might be due to the complete absorption process of molecules and stabilization of C-dots. The complete absorption of molecules has caused the disappearance of the emission peaks from the luminescence spectrum of C-dots. On further decreasing the concentration of Cu2+ ions from 0.001 μM to 5 nM, the existence of excitation peak in the spectrum is clearly displayed. The emission intensity has also displayed enhancement on decreasing the concentration of metal ion in the reaction system. From here, it can be inferred that 0.01 μM was the concentration for the complete absorption and stabilization of C-dots. Further alteration of the concentration of Cu2+ ions produced the incomplete absorption of C-dots.

In case of Cd2+ ions, the enhancement of the emission intensity process has suggested the induction of intrinsic radiative combination decay rate in C-dots. This behavioral variation has mainly aroused due to the increased rate of excitation of C-dots in the presence of Cd2+ ions. The probability of chemical interaction between the localized surface plasmon resonance at the surface of C-dots and Cd2+ ions has also caused the enhancement in the emission peak of C-dots. This enhancement pattern in fluorescence spectroscopy in the presence of C-dots is a very interesting, new, and informative phenomenon (Scheme 1). The intensity behavior has further been tested as a function of concentration of Cd2+ ions in the presence of C-dots (Fig. 7c). The lower concentration range of Cd2+ ions in between 5 μM and 0.001 μM has displayed bifurcated peak of equal emission intensity. These peaks have suggested that energy has been released in two steps. This is mainly due to the existence of extra energy in the system via the complexation of Cd2+ ions with C-dots.

The respective values of fluorescence enhancement factor and fluorescence quenching factor have been calculated for Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions by using normalized emission intensity and are found to be 1.5 and −0.990 (Fig. 7d). The interference effect of different metal ions has also been tested to verify the efficiency of the developed C-dot system in the presence of Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions. For the measurement, 5 μM solutions of different types of metal ions have been prepared using deionized water. To the prepared solution of metal ion, the emission intensity of C-dot has been checked in the presence of Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions. The obtained photoluminescence spectrum has represented that the addition of other metal ions does not affect the efficacy of Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions (Fig. 8 ). The reliability of the developed system has further been checked by doing the same experiment three times. The Stern–Volmer calibration curve has been plotted between F/Fo and concentration of chosen metal ion by using the following mathematical expression (Figs. S4a and b):
F

1 + k

]
where F0 refers to the emission intensity of C-dots without any metal ion, F refers to the emission intensity of C-dots with metal ion, Ksv refers to the Stern–Volmer constant, τ refers to the life span of excited state and Q correspond to the concentration. The detection and quantitative limit of the developed sensor has come out to be 0.18, 0.61 nM for cadmium ion and 0.28, 0.93 nM for copper ion evaluated by applying the standard method of 3σ IUPAC.

In addition, the interacting behavior between metal ions and C-dots has also been calculated in terms of binding constant by applying the Benesi–Hildebrand equation. From the respective method, the value of binding constant has been found to be 0.0139 nM−1 and 0.0058 nM−1 for cadmium and copper, respectively. The linear relationship has further suggested the 1:1 stoichiometry of the developed sensor (Figs. S4c and d). All the above supported and well-matched results of all the experiments have also concluded that our developed sensor displays selective exposure toward Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of developed C-dot sensor in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The recovery studies has been performed by using four different types of water mediums i.e. in the presence of deionized water, tap water, sewage water, and ground water for exploring the practical application of the developed sensor (Fig. 9 ). For the respective study, the different concentrations of Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions were used (10–70 nM) for spiking the water samples taken from different sources in the presence of our C-dots. The obtained results have displayed good agreement in different water resources with our developed sensor. The practical application and recovered value in the range of 92–93% for Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions have verified the potential utility of the sensor for practical purpose. From the respective results, it has also been concluded that our developed system has shown high selectivity for Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions in the presence of ecological water mediums. Furthermore, the developed sensor has an easy working principle. The development of the sensor was quite cheap. The utilization of waste coconut coir has enhanced the scope of the developed sensor. In addition, the developed sensor has provided the green approach for the detection of Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions by using an affordable C-dot based sensor.

The current work has presented the easy and affordable hydrothermal-based method for the synthesis of C-dots with size less than 10 nm from waste coconut coir as the starting precursor source. The different characterization techniques have been used for the detailed structural and physicochemical verification of C-dots. The as-prepared C-dots possessed a quantum yield of 48% and displayed a high solubility rate in aqueous media. The fluorescence emission intensity of C-dots is found to be excitation dependent with high stability toward variation in the reaction medium. The bio-congenial performance of C-dots has been checked by employing quantitative multi-assay including fungus, plant, aquatic animals and at the chromosomal level. The developed system is found to be selective toward cadmium (turn-on) and copper (turn-off) ions due to the enhancement and quenching in the emission intensity of C-dots. The calculated value of limit of detection was found to be 0.18 and 0.28 nM for cadmium and copper ion, respectively, with a regression coefficient of 0.99. The practical utilities of the biocompatible C-dots have been checked over different water resources. The current work provided a simple, easy, bio-matched, harmless, rapid, and cost-effective sensory probe for wastewater remediation.

Pooja Chauhan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data Curation, Writing - Original Draft; Smriti Dogra: Software, Investigation, Data Curation; Savita Chaudhary: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Resources, Writing - Original Draft, Writing - Review & Editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition; Rajeev Kumar: Supervision.
